channel.title: No Spoons Wasted
channel.description: Smart lifehacks for people running on empty. Easy recipes, simple strategies, and helpful products for the spoonie community.

item.title: Weighted Blankets for Chronic Pain Management
item.description:
After a long day moving that involved summer heat, stairs, lifting, bending, carrying, I sat on the couch in a full flare. Even after decades of being a spoonie, it always takes me by surprise. So I pulled up my weighted blanket and felt the relief of the pressure sinking in.

Like many spoonies, I have a strong need to understand how my body works, so I did a little research to understand the power of weighted blankets. Yes, the relief you feel is real. Yes, it has been studied by medical researchers. It doesn’t actually help the pain your body is in, but it does help how intense the pain feels. If you have anxiety on top of pain, it helps even more.

Research shows the weight of the blanket matters, with 15 lbs. being the most helpful. But, weighted blankets are not for every health condition.

Why weighted blankets get a bad wrap

For the longest time, I resisted weighted blankets because I struggled with how to incorporate them into my care routine. They present spoonies with unique challenges.

  • The weight. They are heavy and hard to lug around. If you are already in pain or have no spoons left for the day, it can feel impossible to drag it from couch to bed or wherever you rest.

  • The heat. They can trap in heat and allow it to build up. Heat intolerance is part of my condition, so sleeping with it is off limits.

  • The immobilization. Flares bring fatigue and the weighted blanket can almost immobilize you. Even on days it feels good, it can make it hard to reach for a glass of water, not to mention jumping up to go pee on a moment’s notice.

How research got me to give weighted blankets a second chance

If you are a spoonie who has widespread pain that gets worse from stress and anxiety, research shows that weighted blankets have some real potential for you. I know it helps me, and now I know why.

An article by Baumgartner et. al, published in The Journal of Pain, studied 94 adults using weighted blankets for one week. Here’s what they found:

  • Weighted blankets helped how chronic pain felt.
  • A 15-lb blanket helped more than a 5-lb one.
  • It helped people with high anxiety the most.

It’s important to know that it isn’t reducing your pain in a biological way. There are so many “magic solutions” out there that get our hopes up and then disappoint. But weighted blankets can genuinely reduce the perception of pain. It may not be a cure, and we should be honest about our expectations, but it is a tool that can help on the bad days.

As spoonies, we will take anything that makes it easier to cope with our conditions.

The 7-lb lap blanket for daily usage

I have a weighted lap blanket and I love the versatility. I can use it on my lap when sitting upright at my desk, or on my chest when I’m in my recliner. I can sleep with it without getting too hot. Or, I can drape it over my shoulders when I have neck and back tension. I can use it at work, at home, in the car. I can even travel with it.

The 15-lb full blanket for the bad days

After reading the research that 15lbs is most effective, I decided to rethink the heavier blankets. Having a powerful tool for the bad days always appeals to me, but it takes some consideration. I have to find ways to work around the limitations so I can get the benefits without the hassle.

As spoonies, we are often problem solving things other people don’t ever have to worry about. And that’s ok. It’s what makes us stronger.

Here are the things I had to think through to create a setup that worked for me:.

  • I knew it would be easier to use in winter when trapping in heat is less of a concern. If I wanted to use it in summer, I needed to pair it with something cooling.

  • I wanted to use it for a limited time instead of sleeping with it. So I decided I needed a timer so I didn’t drift off and wake up hot and dehydrated.

  • I need to set up one therapy zone and keep the heavier blanket in that one location so I didn’t have to drag it everywhere.

Here’s my setup

On the bad days, when I’ve overdone it and landed myself in a world of hurt, this is my new set up for reducing how much the pain affects me.

  • 15 lb weighted blanket at the end of the bed that I can easily pull up during therapy sessions.
  • Set a 20 minute timer on my phone so I don’t over do it.
  • Use a clip-on bedside fan to keep my temperature under control.
  • Pair it with this eye messaging mask to enhance the calming effects.
  • Use my favorite app - mindspa - for sound therapy while using it.

Weighted blankets aren’t for everyone

Deep pressure isn’t for every health condition. You should always consult your doctor because we share information to help give you leads to follow-up on responsibility. I am not a doctor, and this is not medical advice.

Here are a few I know for sure shouldn’t use weighted blankets but this list is not exhaustive.

  • People with untreated sleep apnea or significant respiratory issues
  • Anyone with circulation problems serious enough that compression is a concern
  • People with claustrophobia or trauma responses to having movement restricted
  • Mobility limitations where you genuinely can’t get out from under it on your own

And remember, this helps your perception of pain. So if it doesn’t feel good, it’s not helping. It’s not something you should power through because it is “supposed” to help. Always listen to your body.

Bottom line on weighted blankets

Weighted blankets aren’t a cure for chronic pain. The research is clear about that. But the pressure can help calm the pain signals and make the bad days a little easier. If you have anxiety, it has a real shot at helping you.

What I Actually Use

  • Cottonblue Jacquard Weighted Blanket 15lbs: Stylish. Comfy. Good quality.

  • Cottonblue Weighted Lap Blanket 7lbs : This is my every day weighted blanket for pain management

  • RENPHO Eyeris 1 Eye Massager with Heat: It’s a luxury item and I love it. You might not necessarily buy it for yourself but it makes a great spoonie gift.

  • Clip On USB Mini Desk Fan with Emergency Power Bank: Absolutely essential for temperature management.

  • Mindspa: My forever meditation app. Expensive I understand, but worth it for me.

These are affiliate links. As an Amazon Associate I earn from qualifying purchases, at no extra cost to you.

Photo by Amin Hasani on Unsplash

item.title: How to Eat More Protein with Chronic Illness
item.description:
Having a chronic illness is not a permission slip to lay around and eat whatever we want. Far from it. As spoonies we often have to eat even better than the average person to manage our conditions.

When my new doctor found out I had insulin resistance on top of everything else, she asked me to start eating 95 grams of protein a day. My very first thought was a spoonie thought. How can I do this efficiently? I thought I’d just add a hard-boiled egg to my breakfast and call it good.

Until I found out a hard-boiled egg would only add 6 grams of protein to my day. Suddenly what seemed like a simple ask became a mountain to overcome.

Why 95g Is Hard for a Spoonie

The mountain was cooking, one of the hardest spoonie tasks for me. So many high-protein meals require cooking from scratch, and cooking meat in particular can be complex. I don’t have the spoons to do large batch meal prep, so I knew that was out.

The other task stressing me out was tracking. Spoonie brain fog is real. I knew for a fact that tracking protein at every meal was not a possibility. Maybe I could use the spoons to get started, but I wouldn’t be able to sustain it.

In my spoonie life, I have everything balanced. That means I don’t have crashes as often anymore. I live by my routines. But it also means there isn’t room for new things. If I add something, it comes at the expense of something else.

I knew to add more protein, I needed a strategy.

  • New to spoon theory? Here’s the full explainer →

Science Behind Protein for Chronic Illness

The 95g number wasn’t pulled from thin air. Research on protein needs in chronic illness shows that chronically ill people often need significantly more protein than the general population to combat muscle loss and support recovery.

If you want a broader overview, here’s a recent review covering cardiovascular disease, diabetes, inflammation, and muscle preservation.

My Protein Strategy as a Chronically Ill Person

Here’s what is working for me.

Getting my building blocks

I made a list of foods and their protein amounts as my building blocks. Think of this list as your individual Legos that you can combine to create protein-rich meals.

  • Collagen for my coffee (9g)
  • Cottage cheese (13g)
  • Eggs (6g per egg)
  • 6oz of chicken (45g)
  • Hemp hearts (10g)
  • Greek yogurt (15g)
  • Protein shake (30g)
  • Can of tuna (25g per can)
  • Protein chips (10g)
  • Keto bread (6g per slice)
  • Edamame (17g)

Once I had my list, I printed it out and taped it to the inside of my pantry door as reference. There was no way I was going to remember this, and putting it on my phone means I need to know where my phone is, which is a struggle.

Building my protein meals

Then I set a goal. I only eat two meals a day plus a snack, so I knew I needed to combine.

  • A Tuna Salad Sandwich. Tuna + mayo + hemp hearts + keto bread makes a tuna salad sandwich with 47 grams of protein. Add in some protein chips for 10 extra grams and suddenly it didn’t seem so hard.

  • A High-Protein Breakfast. Greek yogurt + hemp hearts + a piece of keto toast + collagen coffee makes a 41-gram breakfast.

  • The No-Spoon Backup. Protein shakes on the days I just can’t are an essential part of my strategy. I’m still looking for the right protein shake, but in a pinch I’m using the Atkins shakes because I don’t have to mix anything to make them happen.

  • If cooking is hard for you too, here’s some advice on cooking with chronic illness.

Why I Refuse to Stress About Protein

Stress makes me worse. Unhelpful internal pressure, guilt of unmet expectations, and stress of keeping up completely tank my nervous system. Avoiding cortisol spikes is one of my spoonie strategies, and I have to balance that with my need to get insulin resistance under control.

Here are some permissions I give myself:

  • Hitting 95g is a goal, but I’m going to celebrate any day I get more protein in, no matter the number. It is still better than where I was.

  • A rough mental count is fine as long as I’m making the effort to add more protein at every meal.

  • Protein shakes are fine on the days I just can’t, and when I find better-tasting, cleaner ones, I’ll swap them out.

  • I hug myself on the days I can’t, because it usually means something else is going on and I don’t have the spoons to do both. Kindness matters.

Community Note

Getting in more protein is a work in progress. I’ll come back and tell you what’s working in a month. If your doctor handed you a number too, let me know what you’re doing. I want to know what other spoonies are figuring out, because we are in this together.

What I Actually Use

  • Great Lakes Wellness Collagen Peptides Powder. My forever collagen. Great taste. Blends effortlessly in coffee.

  • Manitoba Harvest Hemp Hearts. Love the protein and healthy fats. Dump a few tablespoons on nearly anything for a nutty flavor. Works for sweet or savory.

  • Atkins Strong High Protein Shake. Works for now. I don’t love it but I can get it locally and it’s affordable. I would like to replace it with something just as easy with better ingredients.

  • Safe Catch Canned Wild Tuna Fish: My forever tuna. It’s wild caught and tested for lowest mercury. The kind of company I want to support.

  • WILDE Himalayan Pink Salt Protein Chips. Very tasty, natural. Easy way to replace high-carb food with better for you protein.

These are affiliate links. As an Amazon Associate I earn from qualifying purchases, at no extra cost to you.

Photo by Alex Saks on Unsplash

item.title: 2-Ingredient Homemade Jelly That Won't Crash Your Energy
item.description:
In a world where I didn’t have a chronic health condition, I would be a voracious home cook. I love everything about the process. The creativity, making things with my hands, the science, and the delicious reward for all that effort.

Unfortunately, I don’t often have the spoons for elaborate cooking. When I found this 2 ingredient jelly recipe that takes less than 10 minutes and is pretty healthy, I was excited.

It isn’t just healthy and easy, it is emotionally satisfying. I love that I can make something homemade for a change. It fills that home cook longing in me.

  • Need help cooking with chronic illness?

Why I Use Psyllium Fiber As My Gel Base

The magic is in the Psyllium fiber, which is a soluble fiber with lots of viscosity. It slows down the release of glucose into your bloodstream and keeps it stable all the way through digestion. This lessens glucose spikes that can cause spoonie crashes.

Most jelly is made with pectin, which is also a soluble fiber but not really a match for psyllium. Psyllium gives you a thicker gel for a bigger fiber hit and has stronger blood-sugar research behind it.

  • Psyllium fiber improves glycemic control proportional to loss of glycemic control
  • The Effects of Soluble Dietary Fibers on Glycemic Response

Choosing Your Fruit Juice for the Jelly

You can use any fruit juice you want. I use cherry because I like the flavor and it’s hard to find in the store. What’s more important than the flavor is the other ingredients that come along with it. For optimal results:

  • Avoid high fructose corn syrup and other added sugars
  • Pick a berry juice if you want the added benefits of antioxidants

Tart Cherry is naturally high in anthocyanins, flavonoids, and melatonin. Research has shown this can ease inflammation, help with muscle recovery, and support sleep. I always feel good about those added benefits. I’m also interested in trying it with cranberry juice.

If you like things a little tangier, you can add a squeeze of lemon in there. I tend to sprinkle in True Lemon lemon crystals because they are easier to keep around than fresh lemons.

Adjusting the Consistency

Another benefit of making it at home is adjusting the consistency. The thicker the consistency, the more soluble fiber. If you need more glucose control, consider experimenting with a thicker texture. My first attempt was a little soupy, so I had to adjust the recipe for a firmer texture.

Ways to Use It

There are lots of ways to combine this with other healthy foods depending on your dietary needs.

  • Overnight oats: Great if you need added fiber.
  • Cottage cheese: Great if you need more protein like I do.
  • Folded into whipped cream: easy fluffy dessert that adds fiber to all the sugar.
  • Easy ways to eat more protein

What I Actually Use

These are the products I use. You can use whatever juice fits your needs and taste preferences. And since you can make the base whatever you want, there’s a real opportunity to create some fun jelly flavors. What about pineapple cranberry? yum. If you get creative, let me know what you end up liking.

  • Antioxidant Solutions 100% Pure Tart Cherry Juice.
  • NOW Foods Supplements, Psyllium Husk Powder
  • TRUE LEMON Crystallized Lemon

These are affiliate links. As an Amazon Associate I earn from qualifying purchases, at no extra cost to you.

Recipe
